---
title: "Using Code Examples"
---

## How to read the examples

Most chapters include runnable R code interleaved with the explanation. Code
blocks show the call, and the output shown below each block is the actual result
of running it against the book's example data. Where a block builds on an
earlier one, the objects it depends on were created earlier in the same chapter.

The book favors clarity over brevity: intermediate objects are usually named and
inspected rather than chained into a single unbroken pipeline, so that each step
in an analysis is visible and can be checked.

## What the examples assume

The examples use only two families of packages:

- [`tidycreel`](https://chrischizinski.github.io/tidycreel/) — the companion
  package that provides the creel-specific design objects, estimators,
  diagnostics, and plotting helpers.
- The [tidyverse](https://www.tidyverse.org/) — `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`,
  `purrr`, `stringr`, `forcats`, `lubridate`, and `here` for general data
  manipulation and graphics.

A typical chapter begins by loading these:

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(tidycreel)
library(tidyverse)
```

No other packages are required to reproduce the analyses.

## Where the data comes from

The running examples use a small set of example datasets — most prominently the
Harlan Reservoir survey — distributed with the book in `data/` or bundled with
`tidycreel`. The datasets, their structure, and the assumptions behind them are
described in the [Data Notes](appendices/02-data-notes.qmd) appendix. Notation
used across chapters is collected in [Notation and Symbols](appendices/01-notation.qmd).

## Running the code yourself

To run the examples locally, install `tidycreel` and the tidyverse, then work
through a chapter top to bottom. Because later blocks depend on earlier ones,
run a chapter's code in order rather than in isolation. Details on how the book
stays reproducible are in the [Reproducibility](appendices/03-reproducibility.qmd)
appendix.
